{{Short description|none}}

| First game            = {{fb|KEN|colonial}} 1–1  {{fb-rt|UGA|colonial}} <br/> ([[Nairobi]], [[Kenya]]; 1 May 1926)
| Largest win           = {{fb|UGA|colonial}} 13–1 {{fb-rt|KEN|colonial}}<br/>([[Uganda]]; 14 December 1932)
| Largest loss          = {{fb|EGY}} 6–0  {{fb-rt|UGA}}<br/>([[Alexandria, Egypt|Alexandria]], [[Egypt]]; 30 July 1995)<br/>{{fb|TUN}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|UGA}}<br/>([[Tunis]], [[Tunisia]]; 28 February 1999)
| Regional name         = [[Africa Cup of Nations]]
| Regional cup apps     = 8
| Regional cup first    = [[1962 African Cup of Nations|1962]]
| Regional cup best     = Runners-up ([[1978 African Cup of Nations|1978]])
| 2ndRegional name      = [[African Nations Championship]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 6
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2011 African Nations Championship|2011]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = Quarter final ([[2024 African Nations Championship|2024]])
|3rdRegional cup apps=1|3rdRegional name=[[COSAFA Cup]]|3rdRegional cup first=[[2019 COSAFA Cup|2019]]|3rdRegional cup best=Quarter-finals ([[2019 COSAFA Cup|2019]])|4thRegional cup apps=38|4thRegional name=[[CECAFA Cup]]|4thRegional cup first=[[1973 CECAFA Cup|1973]]|4thRegional cup best='''Champions''' ([[1973 CECAFA Cup|1973]], [[1976 CECAFA Cup|1976]], [[1977 CECAFA Cup|1977]], [[1989 CECAFA Cup|1989]], [[1990 CECAFA Cup|1990]], [[1992 CECAFA Cup|1992]], [[1996 CECAFA Cup|1996]], [[2000 CECAFA Cup|2000]], [[2003 CECAFA Cup|2003]], [[2008 CECAFA Cup|2008]], [[2009 CECAFA Cup|2009]], [[2011 CECAFA Cup|2011]], [[2012 CECAFA Cup|2012]], [[2015 CECAFA Cup|2015]], [[2019 CECAFA Cup|2019]])
}}
The '''Uganda national football team''' have represented [[Uganda]] in men's international [[Association football|football]] since the first international match in 1926. It is controlled by [[Federation of Uganda Football Associations]] (FUFA), the governing body for [[football in Uganda]], which is affiliated with [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]] and comes under the global jurisdiction of world football's governing body [[FIFA]]. Uganda competes in the three major international tournaments contested by African nations: the [[FIFA World Cup]], [[Africa Cup of Nations]] and [[African Nations League]].

==History==
The Uganda Cranes made their debut on 1 May 1926 against [[Kenya national football team|Kenya]], drawing 1–1. In 1962, they qualified for their [[Africa Cup of Nations]] debut in the third edition of the tournament, which included only 4 teams. In the semi-finals, the Cranes were defeated and eliminated by the [[United Arab Republic national football team|United Arab Republic]] (2–1), and then lost the third-place match against [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] (3–0).

The Cranes returned to the Africa Cup of Nations in 1974, where they were eliminated in the first round following 2 defeats against [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] and [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]] and a draw against [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]]. In 1976, they were eliminated in the first round, being defeated by [[Ethiopia national football team|Ethiopia]], Egypt and [[Guinea national football team|Guinea]].

In the [[1978 Africa Cup of Nations]], the Cranes finished second in the group stages defeating the [[Congo national football team|Republic of Congo]] (3–1) and [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]] (3–0) and lost 3–1 to Tunisia. In the semi-finals they eliminated Nigeria (2–1), and in the finals they were defeated by [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] (2–0).

In 2017 the Cranes qualified for the African Cup of Nations again after 39 years. They finished the tournament in the first round after two consecutive 1–0 losses to Ghana and Egypt and a 1–1 draw against [[Mali national football team|Mali]].

In the qualifiers for the 2018 World Cup, the Cranes progressed to the second round with a 4–0 aggregate win against [[Togo national football team|Togo]], and were drawn into a group with Egypt, Ghana and the Republic of Congo. They finished the group with 2 0–0 draws against Ghana, a win and a draw against the Republic of Congo and a victory against Egypt followed by a defeat at the home of the Pharaohs. The 9 points won were not enough for Uganda to qualify against the 13 of the Egyptians who finished first in the standings.

In the qualifiers for the [[2019 Africa Cup of Nations]], the Cranes cruised through qualifiers against [[Tanzania national football team|Tanzania]], [[Cape Verde national football team|Cape Verde]] and [[Lesotho national football team|Lesotho]]. In the competition proper, a 2–0 victory against [[DR Congo national football team|DR Congo]] meant that the Uganda Cranes had won their first match in the competition for 41 years. In the other 2 games of the group Uganda obtained a draw against [[Zimbabwe national football team|Zimbabwe]] (1–1) and a defeat against the hosts Egypt (2–0) qualifying in second place, to be eliminated in the round of 16 by [[Senegal national football team|Senegal]] (1–0). They qualified for the [[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]] after [[South Sudan national football team|South Sudan]] beat Congo 3–2 on November 14, 2024.

====Colours====
{{Commons|Uganda national football team kits}}
[[File:Uganda National team Jersey.jpg|thumb|Uganda shirt for 2016]]

Uganda's traditional home colours are red shirts, black shorts and red or black socks. The team has periodically worn an all-red kit.

===Home stadium===
{{Main|Mandela National Stadium}}
[[File:Football Match in Uganda.jpg|thumb|right|Mandela National Stadium prior to redevelopment]]
The Mandela National Stadium was closed for renovation in 2019 after the outbreak of [[COVID-19]] after it failed to reach the minimum standards required by the [[Confederation of African Football|Confederation of African Football (CAF)]] to host international matches.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Muyita |first=Joel |date=2024-04-24 |title=FUFA confirms Uganda Premier League games to be used in soft opening of Mandela National Stadium |url=http://kawowo.com/2024/04/24/fufa-confirms-uganda-premier-league-games-to-be-used-in-soft-opening-of-mandela-national-stadium/ |access-date=2024-08-13 |website=Kawowo Sports |language=en-US}}</ref> In 2020, the stadium was blacklisted from hosting football games due to its substandard condition.<ref name="eng22"/> Reconstruction works, undertaken by the [[Uganda People's Defence Force|UPDF]] Engineering Brigade, began in 2022, and included the installation of a permanent seats in the stadium, new dressing rooms, a modern scoreboard, as well as the refurbishment of floodlights and the pitch.<ref name="eng22">{{cite news |title=Namboole stadium to be ready by June – UPDF Engineering Brigade |url=https://www.independent.co.ug/namboole-stadium-to-be-ready-by-june-updf-engineering-brigade/ |access-date=June 11, 2023 |work=The Independent |date=January 21, 2023}}</ref>
[[File:Namboole Stadium Matchday 02.jpg|thumb|right|The new redeveloped Mandela National Stadium. View from Section 1 with only the expanded roof and seating yet to be completed]]

===Coaching history===
:Interim coaches are listed in ''italics''.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.observer.ug/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=3512%3Arise-and-fall-of-cranes-coaches&Itemid=96 |title=Rise and fall of Cranes coaches |author=Zziwa, Hassan Badru |work=The Observer |location=Uganda |date=25 May 2009 |access-date=29 July 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120323050000/http://www.observer.ug/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=3512%3Arise-and-fall-of-cranes-coaches&Itemid=96 |archive-date=23 March 2012 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://soccer256.com/Rise%20and%20fall%20of%20Uganda%20cranes%20tacticians.htm |title=Rise and fall of Uganda Cranes tacticians |work=Soccer256 online |access-date=29 July 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120202162019/http://soccer256.com/Rise%20and%20fall%20of%20Uganda%20cranes%20tacticians.htm |archive-date=2 February 2012 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.newvision.co.ug/new_vision/news/1507769/johnny-mckinstry-unveiled-uganda-cranes-coach|title=Johnny McKinstry unveiled as Uganda Cranes coach|website=www.newvision.co.ug|access-date=2019-12-30|archive-date=2019-12-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191209201912/https://www.newvision.co.ug/new_vision/news/1507769/johnny-mckinstry-unveiled-uganda-cranes-coach|url-status=live}}</ref>

{{Div col|colwidth=20em}}
* {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Alan Rogers (football manager)|Alan Rogers]] (1965–1966)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Robert Kiberu]] (19??–1969, 1988–1989)
* {{flagicon|FRG}} [[Burkhard Pape]] (1969–1972)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[David Otti]] (1973–1974)
* {{flagicon|FRG}} [[Otto Westerhoff]] (1974–1975)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Peter Okee]] (1976–1981, 1983)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Jaberi Bidandi Ssali]] (1982)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[George Mukasa]] (1984–1985)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Barnabas Mwesiga]] (1986–1988)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Polly Ouma]] (1989–1995)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Timothy Ayieko]] (1995–1996)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Asuman Lubowa]] (1996–1999)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Paul Hasule]] (1999, 2001–2003)
* {{flagicon|NGA}} [[Harrison Okagbue]] (1999–2001)
* {{flagicon|ARG}} [[Pedro Pasculli]] (2003)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Leo Adraa]] (2003–2004)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} [[Mike Mutebi]] (2004)
* {{flagicon|EGY}} [[Mohammed Abbas (football coach)|Mohammed Abbas]] (2004–2006)
* {{flagicon|HUN}} [[Csaba László (footballer born 1964)|Csaba László]] (2006–2008)
* {{flagicon|SCO}} [[Bobby Williamson]] (2008–2013)
* {{flagicon|SRB}} [[Milutin Sredojević]] (2013–2017)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} ''[[Moses Basena]]'' and {{flagicon|UGA}} ''[[Fred Kajoba]]''  (2017)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Sébastien Desabre]] (2017–2019)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} ''[[Abdallah Mubiru]]'' (2019, 2021)
* {{flagicon|NIR}} [[Johnny McKinstry]]  (2019–2021)
* {{flagicon|SRB}} [[Milutin Sredojević]] (2021–2023)
* {{flagicon|UGA}} ''[[Morley Beyekwaso]]'' (2023)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Paul Put]] (2023–present)
*{{div col end}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#International football|friendly]] matches against [[Tanzania]] and [[Madagascar]] on 5 and 8 June 2026, respectively.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://fufa.co.ug/uganda-cranes-28-man-squad-for-international-friendlies-in-morocco/|title=Uganda Cranes 28-Man Squad for International Friendlies in Morocco|publisher=[[Federation of Uganda Football Associations]]|date=23 May 2026|access-date=25 May 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals are correct as of 30 December 2025, after the match against [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Joel Mutakubwa]]|age={{birth date and age|1994|7|17|df=y}}|caps=8|goals=0|club=[[Welwalo Adigrat University FC|Welwalo Adigrat University]]|clubnat=ETH}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up in the last 12 months.
{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Denis Onyango]]|age={{birth date and age|1985|5|15|df=yes}}|caps=84|goals=0|club=[[Mamelodi Sundowns F.C.|Mamelodi Sundowns]]|clubnat=RSA|latest=[[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]]}}

{{nat fs break}}
<!--<sup>DEC</sup> Refused to join the team after the call-up.<br />
<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to an injury.<br />
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad.<br />
<sup>RET</sup> Has retired from international association football.<br />
<sup>SUS</sup> Suspended from the team.<br />
<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew.<br />-->
{{nat fs end}}

===CECAFA Cup===
{{main|CECAFA Cup}}

(1973–2023)

*[[CECAFA Cup]] – '''{{gold1}} Gold medal''' 15 times
*[[CECAFA Cup]] – '''{{silver2}} Silver medal''' 5 times
*[[CECAFA Cup]] – '''{{bronze3}} Bronze medal''' 7 times

== Honours==

===Continental===
* '''[[Africa Cup of Nations|CAF African Cup of Nations]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1978 Africa Cup of Nations|1978]]

===Regional===
* '''[[CECAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (15)''': [[1973 CECAFA Cup|1973]], [[1976 CECAFA Cup|1976]], [[1977 CECAFA Cup|1977]], [[1989 CECAFA Cup|1989]], [[1990 CECAFA Cup|1990]], [[1992 CECAFA Cup|1992]], [[1996 CECAFA Cup|1996]], [[2000 CECAFA Cup|2000]], [[2003 CECAFA Cup|2003]], [[2008 CECAFA Cup|2008]], [[2009 CECAFA Cup|2009]], [[2011 CECAFA Cup|2011]], [[2012 CECAFA Cup|2012]], [[2015 CECAFA Cup|2015]], [[2019 CECAFA Cup|2019]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (5): [[CECAFA Cup|1974]], [[1982 CECAFA Cup|1982]], [[1994 CECAFA Cup|1994]], [[1995 CECAFA Cup|1995]], [[2000 CECAFA Cup|2000]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (7): [[1983 CECAFA Cup|1983]], [[1984 CECAFA Cup|1984]], [[1987 CECAFA Cup|1987]], [[1991 CECAFA Cup|1991]], [[2007 CECAFA Cup|2007]], [[2010 CECAFA Cup|2010]], [[2017 CECAFA Cup|2017]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[CECAFA Cup#Gossage Cup (1926–1966) and Challenge Cup (1967–1971)|Gossage Cup / Challenge Cup]] (25)''': 1928, 1929, 1930, 1932, 1935, 1936, 1937, 1938, 1939, 1940, 1943, 1945, 1947, 1948, 1952, 1954, 1955, 1956, 1957, 1960 (shared), 1962, 1963, 1968, 1969, 1970

===Awards===
* '''[[CAF Awards#African National Team of the Year|African National Team of the Year]] (1)''': 2016
